critic_raw:
The Memory Module for anomaly detection rests on a key representational bottleneck assumption: that limiting CNN capacity will degrade reconstruction quality specifically and proportionally for anomalous inputs. This is not guaranteed and may not hold in practice — a sufficiently expressive memory bank could still reconstruct anomalies if anomalous patterns happen to be interpolable from normal prototypes stored in memory. The paper does not provide ablations that isolate whether the memory capacity itself or the auxiliary losses are the primary driver of anomaly detection performance.

The hybrid abnormality score combining PSNR and L2 loss between queries and memory items is methodologically concerning. PSNR is derived from pixel-level reconstruction error, while the query-memory L2 distance operates in feature space. Combining these two heterogeneous signals without formal justification, principled weighting, or sensitivity analysis raises questions about the robustness of the scoring mechanism. The relative weighting of the two components likely has a substantial effect on detection thresholds and AUC, yet this is rarely ablated systematically.

The Test-Time Updation (TTU) strategy introduces a form of transductive inference — it uses test-time normal frames to update memory items. This creates a problematic methodological conflation: the model is not purely inductive during evaluation. If the test normal-frame selection is imperfect or test-time updating is non-deterministic, performance comparisons against baselines that do not use test-time adaptation become unfair. The paper should clearly separate results with and without TTU to allow apples-to-apples comparisons.

The Separateness and Compactness losses are proposed as novel contributions, but the paper does not adequately demonstrate that they provide improvements beyond reconstruction-only baselines. Without clean ablations showing the incremental value of each loss component, it is impossible to determine whether the memory module itself or the specialized losses drive performance gains. The dual-loss design also introduces additional hyperparameters whose sensitivity is not analyzed.
all_issues_raised: Memory bottleneck assumption unverified: no ablation isolating whether memory capacity or auxiliary losses drive anomaly detection performance
Hybrid abnormality score combines heterogeneous signals (pixel-space PSNR and feature-space L2) without principled weighting or sensitivity analysis
Test-Time Updation conflates inductive and transductive evaluation, making direct comparison to non-adapting baselines unfair without separated results
Separateness and Compactness losses lack clean incremental ablations to justify their contribution over reconstruction-only baselines
No analysis of hyperparameter sensitivity for the dual-loss design or memory bank size, limiting reproducibility claims
all_issues_adjudicated: Memory bottleneck assumption unverified: no ablation isolating whether memory capacity or auxiliary losses drive anomaly detection performance
Hybrid abnormality score combines heterogeneous signals (pixel-space PSNR and feature-space L2) without principled weighting or sensitivity analysis
Test-Time Updation conflates inductive and transductive evaluation, making direct comparison to non-adapting baselines unfair without separated results
Separateness and Compactness losses lack clean incremental ablations to justify their contribution over reconstruction-only baselines
No analysis of hyperparameter sensitivity for the dual-loss design or memory bank size, limiting reproducibility claims

critic_raw:
GAN2Shape's core claim — that 3D shape can be recovered from a pre-trained 2D GAN without any 3D supervision — rests heavily on an implicit assumption: that the StyleGAN2 latent space encodes meaningful, disentangled 3D variation (viewpoint, lighting) as a byproduct of 2D image training. This is an empirical observation about specific GAN architectures and training datasets rather than a proven theoretical property. The method may fail silently when the GAN's latent space does not adequately disentangle these factors, and the paper provides no diagnostic tools or metrics to assess whether this prerequisite holds for a given pretrained model.

The evaluation domains (human faces, cats, cars, buildings) are precisely those for which StyleGAN2 models were trained with large, high-quality datasets. This selection is not representative of the general case and introduces a strong confirmation bias: these are among the categories most likely to have well-structured latent spaces due to the sheer volume and consistency of training data. The paper's claims of generalizability cannot be supported without evaluation on categories where StyleGAN2 training data was more heterogeneous or limited.

The absence of ground-truth 3D evaluation metrics is a significant methodological gap. Without quantitative depth or surface normal accuracy measures against ground truth 3D scans, the paper primarily relies on qualitative visual inspection for 3D shape quality assessment. This makes it impossible to objectively compare recovered 3D shapes against other methods or verify that the recovered geometry is geometrically accurate rather than visually plausible.

The method's reliance on varying latent vector w to generate multi-view training signal introduces a circularity risk: if the latent traversal does not faithfully correspond to true viewpoint variation, the 3D reconstruction will be distorted in systematic, undetectable ways. The paper does not validate that latent traversals genuinely produce accurate perspective-consistent multi-view images before using these as pseudo-ground-truth for 3D learning.
all_issues_raised: Core assumption that StyleGAN2 latent space encodes disentangled 3D variation is treated as given rather than empirically verified per model/dataset
Evaluation restricted to categories (faces, cats, cars, buildings) where StyleGAN2 training data is most abundant and structured, overstating generalizability
Absence of quantitative 3D evaluation against ground-truth geometry; results rely primarily on visual inspection of recovered shapes
Circularity risk: latent w-traversal may not faithfully generate perspective-accurate multi-view images, yet these are used as pseudo-ground-truth for 3D recovery
No diagnostic criteria or metrics for detecting failure cases where the method's 3D assumptions break down
all_issues_adjudicated: Core assumption that StyleGAN2 latent space encodes disentangled 3D variation is treated as given rather than empirically verified per model/dataset
Evaluation restricted to categories (faces, cats, cars, buildings) where StyleGAN2 training data is most abundant and structured, overstating generalizability
Absence of quantitative 3D evaluation against ground-truth geometry; results rely primarily on visual inspection of recovered shapes
Circularity risk: latent w-traversal may not faithfully generate perspective-accurate multi-view images, yet these are used as pseudo-ground-truth for 3D recovery
No diagnostic criteria or metrics for detecting failure cases where the method's 3D assumptions break down

critic_raw:
The privacy-preserving transformation search framework evaluates policies against specific, pre-selected reconstruction attacks. This creates a narrow threat model: policies optimized to defeat gradient inversion attacks of one family may not generalize to adaptive adversaries who adjust their attack strategy after observing the applied augmentations. The paper's claim that policies are 'capable of defeating various types of reconstruction attacks' needs stronger adversarial robustness evaluation, including white-box adaptive attacks where the adversary knows the transformation policy.

The metrics S_pri (privacy score) and S_acc (accuracy score) are novel constructs whose validity relies on assumptions about what constitutes a reconstructible image. If these metrics are not validated against human judgment or existing privacy metrics, they may produce policies that score well numerically but fail to protect privacy in ways that matter (e.g., sensitive attributes are preserved even when pixel reconstruction is imperfect). The paper does not validate that S_pri correlates with human perceptual privacy assessment or with downstream attribute inference attacks.

Transferability of policies across datasets is an important and scientifically valuable claim, but it is vulnerable to dataset shift artifacts. If the evaluated datasets share similar marginal distributions or are drawn from the same data-generating family, apparent transferability may simply reflect this similarity rather than genuine policy generalizability. The paper should demonstrate transferability across datasets with genuinely distinct characteristics (e.g., medical imaging to natural images).

The assertion that effective transformation policies 'obscure details while retaining semantic content' is methodologically circular when the same augmentation-search objective is used to define both privacy and semantic preservation. An independent evaluation of downstream task performance degradation and a separate measurement of privacy leakage (e.g., membership inference, attribute inference) would be needed to validate this claim without circularity.
all_issues_raised: Narrow threat model: policies optimized against specific reconstruction attacks may not generalize to adaptive adversaries who know the transformation policy
S_pri and S_acc metrics are novel constructs not validated against human perceptual privacy judgments or independent attribute-inference attack success rates
Transferability claims are evaluated on datasets that may share distributional characteristics, overstating generalizability across genuinely distinct domains
Claim that policies retain semantic content while obscuring details is methodologically circular when the same objective defines both properties
No evaluation of membership inference or attribute inference attacks, which are complementary and often more practical privacy threats than reconstruction attacks
all_issues_adjudicated: Narrow threat model: policies optimized against specific reconstruction attacks may not generalize to adaptive adversaries who know the transformation policy
S_pri and S_acc metrics are novel constructs not validated against human perceptual privacy judgments or independent attribute-inference attack success rates
Transferability claims are evaluated on datasets that may share distributional characteristics, overstating generalizability across genuinely distinct domains
Claim that policies retain semantic content while obscuring details is methodologically circular when the same objective defines both properties
No evaluation of membership inference or attribute inference attacks, which are complementary and often more practical privacy threats than reconstruction attacks

critic_raw:
The unrolled network for phase retrieval introduces a learned reference image as a critical component. However, the claim that this reference generalizes to unseen data distributions is methodologically underspecified: 'generalization' here depends on how much the unseen distribution differs from the training distribution. The paper validates generalization on open-access datasets that are likely related to or overlapping with the training regime. A stronger evaluation would test on datasets with genuinely different imaging modalities or signal statistics to characterize the scope of generalization.

The claim of robustness to spatial data augmentations such as shifting and rotation is algorithmically significant but the experimental protocol for this test is underspecified. It is unclear whether the augmented variants are applied at test time or during training, what the magnitude and distribution of transformations were, and whether the performance degradation under augmentation is compared against a principled baseline (e.g., a classical phase retrieval algorithm). Without this detail, the robustness claim cannot be independently verified.

The unrolled network architecture unrolls a fixed number of iterations of the iterative algorithm. This introduces a hyperparameter (number of unrolling steps) whose impact on both solution quality and generalization is not systematically analyzed. Unrolled networks are known to exhibit sharp performance transitions as a function of unrolling depth, and the selected depth may be overfit to the evaluation datasets.

Validation using 'original code and open-access datasets' is presented as a strength, but if the open-access datasets were also used by the original authors during development or hyperparameter selection, this validation is not truly held-out. The paper should clarify the independence of the validation datasets from the original paper's development process to establish genuine out-of-sample performance.
all_issues_raised: Generalization to unseen distributions is evaluated on datasets likely similar to training data; genuinely out-of-distribution evaluation is absent
Robustness to spatial augmentations (shift, rotation) is underspecified: augmentation protocol, magnitude, and comparison baseline are not detailed
Number of unrolling steps is a critical hyperparameter whose sensitivity and potential overfitting to evaluation datasets is not analyzed
Validation datasets may not be independent of original authors' development process, undermining held-out evaluation claims
No comparison against classical iterative phase retrieval algorithms with matched computational budget to establish that learning the reference genuinely outperforms non-learned alternatives
all_issues_adjudicated: Generalization to unseen distributions is evaluated on datasets likely similar to training data; genuinely out-of-distribution evaluation is absent
Robustness to spatial augmentations (shift, rotation) is underspecified: augmentation protocol, magnitude, and comparison baseline are not detailed
Number of unrolling steps is a critical hyperparameter whose sensitivity and potential overfitting to evaluation datasets is not analyzed
Validation datasets may not be independent of original authors' development process, undermining held-out evaluation claims
No comparison against classical iterative phase retrieval algorithms with matched computational budget to establish that learning the reference genuinely outperforms non-learned alternatives

critic_raw:
Hamiltonian Generative Networks claim to learn Hamiltonian dynamics from image sequences without restrictive domain assumptions. However, the evaluation systems (pendulum, body-spring, two/three bodies) all involve conservative systems with relatively simple, periodic dynamics and low-dimensional phase spaces. These represent a very narrow and favorable subset of physical systems. The absence of evaluation on dissipative systems, chaotic systems, or systems with non-standard Hamiltonians fundamentally limits the scope of the 'no restrictive domain assumptions' claim.

The variational model's ability to disentangle position and momentum from raw image sequences requires strong implicit assumptions about temporal regularity and smooth dynamics. The paper does not rigorously characterize what assumptions are actually made at the architecture level versus what is learned from data. This conflation makes it difficult to assess whether the approach truly learns Hamiltonian structure or instead exploits inductive biases baked into the architecture that happen to resemble Hamiltonian dynamics for the tested systems.

The evaluation of rollout quality relies on visual comparison of reconstructed versus ground-truth image sequences. Without quantitative metrics for trajectory accuracy in latent phase space (not just pixel reconstruction), it is unclear whether the learned Hamiltonian captures physically meaningful invariants (e.g., energy conservation) or merely produces visually similar image sequences through learned interpolation.

Backward time rollout is presented as evidence of Hamiltonian learning (Hamiltonians are time-reversible), but this test is insufficient: any autoregressive model can generate backward trajectories if trained with reversed sequences. The paper should demonstrate that the model's backward rollout accuracy is genuinely superior to a non-Hamiltonian baseline trained with the same data, and that the energy of rollout trajectories is approximately conserved over long horizons.
all_issues_raised: Evaluation restricted to simple conservative systems (pendulum, spring-body, N-body); dissipative, chaotic, or irregular systems are absent, limiting domain generality claims
Conflation of architecture-level inductive biases with learned Hamiltonian structure; paper does not clearly separate what is assumed versus what is learned
Rollout quality evaluated primarily via pixel reconstruction; no quantitative phase-space trajectory accuracy or energy conservation metrics
Backward time rollout does not uniquely validate Hamiltonian learning since non-Hamiltonian models can also generate reversed sequences when trained appropriately
No comparison to non-Hamiltonian latent dynamics baselines (e.g., standard VAE with recurrent dynamics) to isolate the contribution of Hamiltonian structure
all_issues_adjudicated: Evaluation restricted to simple conservative systems (pendulum, spring-body, N-body); dissipative, chaotic, or irregular systems are absent, limiting domain generality claims
Conflation of architecture-level inductive biases with learned Hamiltonian structure; paper does not clearly separate what is assumed versus what is learned
Rollout quality evaluated primarily via pixel reconstruction; no quantitative phase-space trajectory accuracy or energy conservation metrics
Backward time rollout does not uniquely validate Hamiltonian learning since non-Hamiltonian models can also generate reversed sequences when trained appropriately
No comparison to non-Hamiltonian latent dynamics baselines (e.g., standard VAE with recurrent dynamics) to isolate the contribution of Hamiltonian structure

critic_raw:
The Graph Edit Network (GEN) is evaluated exclusively on synthetic datasets generated specifically for this purpose. Synthetic datasets allow precise control over graph properties but introduce a fundamental external validity problem: they may not reflect the complexity, noise, and structural variability of real-world graph transformation tasks. Without evaluation on at least one real-world graph editing or transformation benchmark, claims about GEN's practical applicability are unsupported.

The paper's emphasis on scalability to larger graphs is presented as a strength, but the evidence for scalability is limited by whatever graph sizes were included in the synthetic evaluation. If the largest evaluated graphs are still relatively small (e.g., tens to low hundreds of nodes), the computational complexity claims lack empirical support at scales relevant to real applications such as program graphs, molecular graphs, or knowledge graphs, which can contain thousands of nodes.

GEN is framed as a general output layer for graph neural networks, but the evaluation appears to test GEN in specific task configurations rather than demonstrating its plug-and-play compatibility with diverse GNN backbones. Without experiments showing GEN's performance when combined with multiple distinct GNN architectures (e.g., GCN, GAT, GraphSAGE), the claim of architectural generality is speculative.

The paper does not clearly specify how the edit operations (node/edge insertions, deletions, attribute changes) are learned jointly versus independently, or how conflicts between simultaneously predicted edit operations are resolved. In practice, graph editing is non-commutative and order-dependent; the paper should clarify whether GEN produces edit sequences (ordered) or edit sets (unordered) and what assumptions are made about operation independence.
all_issues_raised: Evaluation exclusively on synthetic datasets limits external validity; no real-world graph transformation benchmark is included
Scalability claims lack empirical support at graph sizes relevant to real applications (molecular, program, or knowledge graphs with thousands of nodes)
Generality as a GNN output layer is not demonstrated across multiple distinct GNN backbones; compatibility with diverse architectures is assumed, not tested
Non-commutativity and order-dependence of graph edit operations is not addressed; paper is unclear whether GEN produces ordered sequences or unordered edit sets
Synthetic dataset generation procedure may inadvertently encode structural biases that favor GEN's specific editing formulation, inflating performance estimates
all_issues_adjudicated: Evaluation exclusively on synthetic datasets limits external validity; no real-world graph transformation benchmark is included
Scalability claims lack empirical support at graph sizes relevant to real applications (molecular, program, or knowledge graphs with thousands of nodes)
Generality as a GNN output layer is not demonstrated across multiple distinct GNN backbones; compatibility with diverse architectures is assumed, not tested
Non-commutativity and order-dependence of graph edit operations is not addressed; paper is unclear whether GEN produces ordered sequences or unordered edit sets
Synthetic dataset generation procedure may inadvertently encode structural biases that favor GEN's specific editing formulation, inflating performance estimates

critic_raw:
Generative Causal Explanations (GCE) for black-box classifiers relies on a fundamental assumption: that the latent space of the generative model can be partitioned into causal and non-causal factors that correspond meaningfully to the classifier's decision boundary. This assumption is not verified during training — it is operationalized through the learning objective, creating a circularity where the model learns to label its own latent dimensions as causal by optimizing an objective that is defined using those same dimensions. Without independent causal graph validation or interventional experiments, the 'causal' label is largely a naming convention.

The evaluation on MNIST and fMNIST datasets involves classifiers operating on images where class-discriminative features are relatively obvious and well-understood. This makes the evaluation setup favorable to causal explanation methods: the 'causal' factors identified by GCE (e.g., digit shape vs. background) align with human intuitions on these clean datasets. The method's ability to identify non-obvious or confounded causal factors in more realistic, noisy datasets is not demonstrated.

The description of Algorithm 1 implementation lacks detail on key reproducibility-critical choices: learning rate schedules, architectural specifications for the generative model, the number of causal latent dimensions, and the procedure for separating causal from non-causal factors at inference time. Specifying hardware (GTX 1080) without specifying software versions, random seeds, and exact hyperparameters is an incomplete reproducibility disclosure.

The 'black-box' framing of the target classifier is methodologically important but the extent of black-box access assumed during the explanation procedure is not precisely defined. If the GCE model requires gradient access to the classifier (which is common in GAN-based explanation methods), the method is not truly black-box but rather gray-box, which would misrepresent its practical applicability to scenarios where only prediction access is available.
all_issues_raised: Circularity in causal/non-causal partitioning: the model self-labels latent dimensions as causal through the same objective that defines causality, without independent validation
Evaluation restricted to clean, well-understood datasets (MNIST, fMNIST) where causal factors align with human intuitions; harder, confounded scenarios absent
Incomplete reproducibility disclosure: hardware specified but learning rate schedules, architectural details, random seeds, and hyperparameters are not fully documented
Black-box access assumption is imprecisely defined; if gradient access to classifier is required, the method is gray-box and the black-box framing is misleading
No interventional experiment or independent causal graph validation to confirm that identified 'causal' factors correspond to true causal relationships
all_issues_adjudicated: Circularity in causal/non-causal partitioning: the model self-labels latent dimensions as causal through the same objective that defines causality, without independent validation
Evaluation restricted to clean, well-understood datasets (MNIST, fMNIST) where causal factors align with human intuitions; harder, confounded scenarios absent
Incomplete reproducibility disclosure: hardware specified but learning rate schedules, architectural details, random seeds, and hyperparameters are not fully documented
Black-box access assumption is imprecisely defined; if gradient access to classifier is required, the method is gray-box and the black-box framing is misleading
No interventional experiment or independent causal graph validation to confirm that identified 'causal' factors correspond to true causal relationships

critic_raw:
YNet's two-stage prediction framework (goal prediction followed by waypoint-conditioned path prediction) introduces a sequential error propagation problem: errors in the first stage (goal prediction) compound into errors in the second stage (path prediction from sampled waypoints). The paper does not report an oracle experiment using ground-truth goal locations to characterize the upper bound on path accuracy that would be achievable if goal prediction were perfect. Without this baseline, it is impossible to determine whether path prediction quality or goal prediction quality is the primary performance bottleneck.

The use of segmentation maps as environmental context is an important architectural choice that introduces a practical limitation: YNet requires accurate semantic segmentation as input at inference time. Real-world trajectory forecasting scenarios often do not have high-quality segmentation maps available, and the paper does not evaluate performance under degraded or noisy segmentation inputs. This dependency on segmentation quality is underreported and limits deployment generalizability.

The Stanford Drone Dataset (SDD) and ETH/UCY benchmarks have known evaluation protocol variations that affect comparability across papers. Different works use different train/test splits, different spatial scales, different FDE/ADE computation horizons, and different numbers of samples for multi-modal evaluation. The paper should explicitly specify its evaluation protocol and demonstrate that comparison baselines were evaluated under identical conditions.

The multi-modal trajectory prediction is evaluated using best-of-K sample metrics (minADE, minFDE), which are sensitive to the number of samples K. This metric can be gamed by generating many diverse samples and selecting the best, and comparisons are only valid when all methods use the same K. The paper's handling of the K parameter across all comparison methods must be transparent.
all_issues_raised: No oracle experiment with ground-truth goal locations to characterize the ceiling on path accuracy, preventing identification of the primary performance bottleneck
Dependency on high-quality semantic segmentation maps at inference time is a significant practical limitation not evaluated under degraded segmentation conditions
SDD and ETH/UCY benchmarks have protocol variations (splits, scale, horizon, sample count) that affect cross-paper comparability; protocol specification is critical
Best-of-K evaluation metrics (minADE, minFDE) are sensitive to K and can be gamed; comparability across baselines requires identical K values which may not be enforced
Sequential error propagation from goal to path prediction stage is not analyzed; the contribution of each stage to final forecast error is not decomposed
all_issues_adjudicated: No oracle experiment with ground-truth goal locations to characterize the ceiling on path accuracy, preventing identification of the primary performance bottleneck
Dependency on high-quality semantic segmentation maps at inference time is a significant practical limitation not evaluated under degraded segmentation conditions
SDD and ETH/UCY benchmarks have protocol variations (splits, scale, horizon, sample count) that affect cross-paper comparability; protocol specification is critical
Best-of-K evaluation metrics (minADE, minFDE) are sensitive to K and can be gamed; comparability across baselines requires identical K values which may not be enforced
Sequential error propagation from goal to path prediction stage is not analyzed; the contribution of each stage to final forecast error is not decomposed

critic_raw:
Strategic classification frameworks require a precise game-theoretic specification of how users respond to classifiers. The paper claims its framework accommodates 'strategic and non-strategic users' but the behavioral model for user responses must be specified before any theoretical or empirical guarantees can be established. If the user response model is misspecified — for instance, assuming best-response behavior when real users respond heuristically — the framework's performance guarantees break down. The paper should validate user response model assumptions against empirical user behavior data rather than relying on theoretically convenient but behaviorally untested response functions.

The regularization strategies intended to 'promote favorable social outcomes' are normatively loaded: what constitutes a 'favorable social outcome' varies by context and stakeholder perspective. The paper must operationalize social welfare objectives precisely (e.g., social welfare maximization, Pareto improvement, individual vs. aggregate benefit) and justify its chosen operationalization. Without this, the 'promotes favorable social outcomes' claim is not falsifiable.

The framework's claimed practical utility is contingent on differentiability through user responses, which requires that the response function be differentiable with respect to the model parameters. For discontinuous or threshold-based user response functions (which are common in real strategic settings), this differentiability assumption fails and the end-to-end training approach breaks down. The paper should clearly characterize the class of response functions for which its approach is valid.

Performance comparison against non-strategic approaches is presented as a key validation, but the experimental setup must ensure that the strategic approach does not benefit from additional computational budget or model capacity relative to non-strategic baselines. If the strategic classifier uses a more powerful model or more training data to accommodate the end-to-end training objective, performance gains may reflect capacity differences rather than the strategic learning framework itself.
all_issues_raised: User response model assumptions are not validated against empirical behavioral data; theoretical convenience of response functions may not reflect real strategic behavior
Social outcome objectives are normatively underspecified; 'favorable social outcomes' is not operationalized in a falsifiable, context-appropriate way
Differentiability requirement for end-to-end training fails for discontinuous or threshold-based user response functions common in real strategic settings
Comparison against non-strategic baselines may not control for model capacity or training budget differences, confounding the strategic learning contribution
Framework's behavior in mixed populations (strategic and non-strategic users simultaneously) may not match claimed performance in purely strategic or purely non-strategic scenarios
all_issues_adjudicated: User response model assumptions are not validated against empirical behavioral data; theoretical convenience of response functions may not reflect real strategic behavior
Social outcome objectives are normatively underspecified; 'favorable social outcomes' is not operationalized in a falsifiable, context-appropriate way
Differentiability requirement for end-to-end training fails for discontinuous or threshold-based user response functions common in real strategic settings
Comparison against non-strategic baselines may not control for model capacity or training budget differences, confounding the strategic learning contribution
Framework's behavior in mixed populations (strategic and non-strategic users simultaneously) may not match claimed performance in purely strategic or purely non-strategic scenarios

critic_raw:
The ATSPrivacy framework's central claim is that data augmentation policies can protect privacy against gradient reconstruction attacks while maintaining model accuracy. However, the search algorithm optimizes S_pri and S_acc jointly, and the relationship between these proxy metrics and actual privacy/utility guarantees is not formally characterized. A policy that achieves high S_pri scores may still leak sensitive information through auxiliary channels (attribute inference, membership inference) not captured by the reconstruction-focused S_pri metric.

The framework's defense mechanism is evaluated against a fixed set of reconstruction attack implementations. In an adaptive adversarial setting where the attacker knows which augmentation policies are being applied, the attacker can adapt their reconstruction algorithm to invert or account for known transformations (e.g., undoing contrast adjustments). The paper does not evaluate against such white-box adaptive attacks, which would more accurately reflect the threat model in real federated learning deployments.

The search algorithm explores a combinatorial space of augmentation policies, but the search procedure's convergence guarantees and sensitivity to the search space definition (which augmentations are included and excluded) are not analyzed. If the found policies are highly sensitive to minor changes in the search space definition, the framework cannot be reliably deployed in new settings without re-running the expensive search procedure.

The evaluation of privacy protection measures how much a reconstruction attacker can recover. But this conflates perceptual image reconstruction quality with privacy leakage: an attack that produces blurry but structurally accurate reconstructions may still reveal sensitive attributes (identity, medical condition, location). The paper should supplement pixel-level reconstruction metrics with downstream attribute inference or re-identification attack evaluations to provide a more complete privacy assessment.
all_issues_raised: S_pri and S_acc proxy metrics may not capture privacy leakage through attribute inference or membership inference attacks beyond gradient reconstruction
Defense not evaluated against adaptive white-box attacks where adversary knows the applied augmentation policy and can adjust reconstruction accordingly
Search algorithm convergence and sensitivity to search space definition are unanalyzed, limiting reliable deployment in new federated learning configurations
Pixel-level reconstruction metrics conflate perceptual image quality with privacy; attribute inference and re-identification risks are not separately evaluated
Joint optimization of privacy and accuracy scores may yield Pareto-suboptimal policies; no analysis of the privacy-utility tradeoff frontier is provided
all_issues_adjudicated: S_pri and S_acc proxy metrics may not capture privacy leakage through attribute inference or membership inference attacks beyond gradient reconstruction
Defense not evaluated against adaptive white-box attacks where adversary knows the applied augmentation policy and can adjust reconstruction accordingly
Search algorithm convergence and sensitivity to search space definition are unanalyzed, limiting reliable deployment in new federated learning configurations
Pixel-level reconstruction metrics conflate perceptual image quality with privacy; attribute inference and re-identification risks are not separately evaluated
Joint optimization of privacy and accuracy scores may yield Pareto-suboptimal policies; no analysis of the privacy-utility tradeoff frontier is provided

critic_raw:
The projection-based truncated SVD update algorithm's core efficiency claim is that it avoids full matrix decompositions by updating only necessary components. However, the computational complexity analysis must account for the projection operations themselves, which may have non-trivial cost depending on matrix rank, update rank, and projection dimensionality. The paper should provide explicit complexity bounds (in terms of matrix dimensions m, n, rank k, and update rank r) and compare these against both full recomputation and other incremental SVD methods under the same asymptotic assumptions.

The accuracy of the truncated SVD update is subject to approximation error that accumulates over successive updates. The paper does not characterize how approximation error grows as a function of the number of sequential updates applied to the same matrix, or at what frequency a full recomputation would be necessary to prevent unbounded error accumulation. Without this analysis, the method cannot be safely deployed in streaming or long-running dynamic systems.

The baseline comparisons use 'baseline methods' but the paper does not specify which established incremental SVD methods are included in the comparison. The literature contains several incremental SVD methods (e.g., Brand's method, Bunch-Nielsen-Sorensen updates, randomized methods) with different computational profiles. If the comparisons exclude faster or more established competing methods, the performance claims are overstated.

The theoretical analyses and empirical comparisons are described together without clear separation. Strong papers in computational linear algebra separate (1) theoretical complexity bounds, (2) numerical stability analysis, and (3) empirical wall-clock performance. The paper should clearly distinguish what is proven theoretically versus what is observed empirically, particularly for speed and resource utilization claims.
all_issues_raised: Computational complexity bounds for projection operations are not explicitly derived, making efficiency claims relative to full recomputation unverifiable
Approximation error accumulation across successive updates is not characterized; no guidance on when full recomputation is necessary to control error growth
Baseline comparison set is underspecified; exclusion of established incremental SVD methods (Brand's, BNS, randomized) may overstate performance improvements
Theoretical complexity analysis, numerical stability analysis, and empirical benchmarks are not clearly separated, conflating proven and observed claims
Evaluation matrix sizes and update rank configurations may not reflect practical dynamic systems workloads, limiting real-world performance claims
all_issues_adjudicated: Computational complexity bounds for projection operations are not explicitly derived, making efficiency claims relative to full recomputation unverifiable
Approximation error accumulation across successive updates is not characterized; no guidance on when full recomputation is necessary to control error growth
Baseline comparison set is underspecified; exclusion of established incremental SVD methods (Brand's, BNS, randomized) may overstate performance improvements
Theoretical complexity analysis, numerical stability analysis, and empirical benchmarks are not clearly separated, conflating proven and observed claims
Evaluation matrix sizes and update rank configurations may not reflect practical dynamic systems workloads, limiting real-world performance claims

critic_raw:
The StylEx approach (explaining classifiers in StyleSpace) relies on a key assumption: that the StyleGAN2 StyleSpace contains semantically meaningful and disentangled dimensions that correspond to classifier-relevant attributes. This assumption is dataset- and domain-specific. StyleGAN2 trained on faces may exhibit well-disentangled style dimensions because faces have strong regularities, but the same assumption does not hold for more heterogeneous or less structured domains. The paper's claims about explaining classifiers in arbitrary domains are limited by this architecture-specific disentanglement assumption.

The framework integrates a pre-trained StyleGAN2 with a fully trained classifier, but the compatibility between the two components is not guaranteed. The classifier may use features that are not represented in StyleGAN2's latent space (e.g., texture statistics captured by classifier but not encoded in style coefficients), leading to explanations that systematically miss certain classifier-relevant factors. The paper should evaluate whether the explanations are complete — i.e., whether all factors influencing the classifier's decisions are capturable through StyleSpace manipulations.

Counterfactual explanations generated by StyleSpace traversal are evaluated by whether they change the classifier's prediction, but this is a necessary but not sufficient condition for explanation quality. A traversal that changes the prediction by flipping many style dimensions simultaneously is less informative than one that identifies the minimal set of dimensions required. The paper should report the number of style dimensions modified per explanation and demonstrate that explanations are minimal.

The replication study must grapple with the inherent stochasticity of GAN training and the sensitivity of StyleSpace disentanglement to random seeds and training configurations. Small changes in GAN initialization can substantially alter which style dimensions encode which semantic attributes, making replication of specific findings (e.g., 'dimension 42 encodes nose shape') difficult to reproduce across different training runs.
all_issues_raised: StyleSpace disentanglement assumption is architecture- and domain-specific; claims about explaining classifiers in arbitrary domains are unjustified
Explanation completeness is not verified: classifier-relevant features not representable in StyleSpace would be systematically invisible to the explanation method
Counterfactual quality is evaluated by prediction change, not explanation minimality; multi-dimensional traversals may produce confounded, uninformative explanations
GAN training stochasticity means specific style dimension semantics may not replicate across training runs, undermining the replication study's conclusions
No user study or human-grounded evaluation of whether StyleSpace-derived explanations are interpretable and actionable by non-expert users
all_issues_adjudicated: StyleSpace disentanglement assumption is architecture- and domain-specific; claims about explaining classifiers in arbitrary domains are unjustified
Explanation completeness is not verified: classifier-relevant features not representable in StyleSpace would be systematically invisible to the explanation method
Counterfactual quality is evaluated by prediction change, not explanation minimality; multi-dimensional traversals may produce confounded, uninformative explanations
GAN training stochasticity means specific style dimension semantics may not replicate across training runs, undermining the replication study's conclusions
No user study or human-grounded evaluation of whether StyleSpace-derived explanations are interpretable and actionable by non-expert users

critic_raw:
DECAF's causal debiasing relies on a user-provided directed acyclic graph (DAG) that encodes assumed causal relationships in the dataset. This DAG is a strong structural assumption that must be specified before training. If the assumed DAG is misspecified — edges are incorrect, confounders are omitted, or the wrong variables are designated as sensitive — the debiasing procedure may produce synthetic data that appears fair by its own metrics but retains or introduces causal bias along pathways not captured in the DAG. The paper should include robustness analysis to DAG misspecification.

Fairness evaluation in DECAF measures whether downstream models trained on the synthetic data meet fairness criteria. However, downstream model architecture and training procedure are design choices that interact with the synthetic data in complex ways. If a fixed downstream model architecture is used for all evaluations, the fairness results may be specific to that architecture and not generalizable. The paper should evaluate fairness across multiple downstream model architectures to establish that the debiasing is robust to the choice of downstream model.

The GAN training objective in DECAF combines adversarial loss with causal constraint terms. GAN training is notoriously unstable, and adding causal constraints may exacerbate instability by introducing competing gradient signals. The paper should report training stability metrics and demonstrate that the optimization reliably converges to solutions that satisfy both the adversarial (realism) and causal (fairness) objectives simultaneously.

The preprocessing category of fairness approaches, which DECAF belongs to, faces a fundamental evaluation challenge: fairness on the synthetic data does not guarantee fairness on real-world deployment data if the synthetic and real data distributions differ. The paper should characterize the distribution shift between original and synthetic data using appropriate divergence measures and analyze how this shift affects downstream model performance on held-out real data.
all_issues_raised: DECAF requires a pre-specified causal DAG; misspecification of causal structure may produce synthetic data that retains bias along unmodeled causal pathways
Fairness evaluation uses a fixed downstream model architecture; results may not generalize across different downstream model families
GAN training instability may be exacerbated by competing adversarial and causal constraint objectives; convergence reliability is not reported
Distribution shift between original and synthetic data is not characterized; fairness guarantees on synthetic data may not transfer to real deployment data
Fairness metrics used for evaluation may not capture all relevant dimensions of bias (e.g., intersectional fairness, counterfactual fairness beyond the modeled DAG)
all_issues_adjudicated: DECAF requires a pre-specified causal DAG; misspecification of causal structure may produce synthetic data that retains bias along unmodeled causal pathways
Fairness evaluation uses a fixed downstream model architecture; results may not generalize across different downstream model families
GAN training instability may be exacerbated by competing adversarial and causal constraint objectives; convergence reliability is not reported
Distribution shift between original and synthetic data is not characterized; fairness guarantees on synthetic data may not transfer to real deployment data
Fairness metrics used for evaluation may not capture all relevant dimensions of bias (e.g., intersectional fairness, counterfactual fairness beyond the modeled DAG)

critic_raw:
The 'Rigging the Lottery' (RigL) methodology claims to make all initialization configurations lead to successful training outcomes by manipulating network initialization and training regimen. However, the claim of universal applicability is empirically falsifiable only through systematic evaluation across a representative diversity of architectures, datasets, and task types. If the experimental evaluation covers a limited range of architectures (e.g., only ResNet variants) and standard vision datasets, the universality claim is overstated relative to the evidence presented.

Statistical analyses claiming improvements over standard initialization techniques require careful attention to multiple comparison correction and variance reporting. Sparse training methods are highly sensitive to random seeds due to the stochastic nature of mask updates, and the reported improvements may not be statistically significant with appropriate multiple hypothesis correction across architectures and dataset combinations. The paper should report variance across multiple independent runs and apply appropriate correction for multiple comparisons.

The term 'lottery ticket' is used metaphorically to describe initialization configurations. The paper's relationship to the original Lottery Ticket Hypothesis (Frankle & Carlin 2019) should be precisely characterized: does RigL operate in the same theoretical framework, or does it reuse the metaphor while operating under different assumptions? If RigL's 'tickets' are fundamentally different from Frankle & Carlin's subnetworks, comparisons to that framework may be misleading.

The training regimen modifications introduce additional computational costs (e.g., gradient computation for masked weights at each update step). The paper should provide wall-clock time comparisons in addition to FLOP-count comparisons, as the practical training overhead of the proposed method relative to dense baselines may differ significantly from the theoretical FLOP reduction, particularly on GPU hardware where sparse operations are not always computationally efficient.
all_issues_raised: Universal applicability claims exceed the evidence if evaluation covers a limited range of architectures and standard vision datasets
Variance and statistical significance across multiple seeds are not adequately reported; sparse training methods are highly seed-sensitive
Relationship to original Lottery Ticket Hypothesis is not precisely characterized; 'lottery ticket' metaphor may mislead readers about theoretical compatibility
Wall-clock training time comparisons are absent; FLOP reductions may not translate to practical speedups due to GPU sparse operation overhead
Multiple comparison correction is likely not applied across the full set of architecture-dataset combinations evaluated, inflating apparent significance of improvements
all_issues_adjudicated: Universal applicability claims exceed the evidence if evaluation covers a limited range of architectures and standard vision datasets
Variance and statistical significance across multiple seeds are not adequately reported; sparse training methods are highly seed-sensitive
Relationship to original Lottery Ticket Hypothesis is not precisely characterized; 'lottery ticket' metaphor may mislead readers about theoretical compatibility
Wall-clock training time comparisons are absent; FLOP reductions may not translate to practical speedups due to GPU sparse operation overhead
Multiple comparison correction is likely not applied across the full set of architecture-dataset combinations evaluated, inflating apparent significance of improvements

critic_raw:
Counterfactual Generative Networks (CGN) condition a GAN generator on counterfactual contexts sampled from 'rich prior knowledge sources.' The nature and quality of these prior knowledge sources are critical: if they are themselves biased, incomplete, or poorly calibrated probability distributions, the generated counterfactuals will reflect these biases and may not constitute meaningful counterfactual interventions from a causal standpoint. The paper should characterize what prior knowledge sources are used, how they were constructed, and how sensitive the generation quality is to the quality of these priors.

The evaluation using Inception Score (IS) and Frechet Inception Distance (FID) is standard for generative model evaluation, but these metrics measure distributional similarity to the training data and may not capture counterfactual validity. A generated image that is IS/FID-optimal may fail to be a faithful counterfactual intervention — it may change many attributes simultaneously rather than the minimal set required by the counterfactual query. The paper lacks evaluation metrics specifically designed to assess counterfactual fidelity (e.g., attribute-specific classification accuracy on the manipulated attribute, consistency of non-manipulated attributes).

The discriminator is designed to evaluate authenticity of generated samples 'against these conditions,' but the conditioning structure of the discriminator must be precisely specified. If the discriminator is simply a conditional discriminator that receives the counterfactual query as input, it may learn to ignore the query and focus on image realism. The paper should verify that the discriminator genuinely enforces counterfactual-query consistency rather than merely unconditional realism.

Diversity and realism are presented as dual improvements, but diversity can be superficially inflated by introducing random variation that is not causally meaningful. The paper should demonstrate that the diversity of generated samples reflects genuine variation along the counterfactual dimension specified in the query, not spurious variation in unrelated attributes.
all_issues_raised: Prior knowledge sources for counterfactual conditioning are underspecified; their quality and potential biases directly affect the causal validity of generated counterfactuals
IS and FID metrics evaluate distributional similarity, not counterfactual fidelity; specific metrics for counterfactual attribute consistency are absent
Discriminator conditioning on counterfactual queries may be superficial; the paper does not verify that the discriminator genuinely enforces query-consistent generation
Diversity improvements may reflect spurious variation in non-counterfactual attributes rather than meaningful variation along the causally intervened dimension
The causal framework underpinning 'counterfactual' generation is not formally specified; without a structural causal model, the counterfactual label may be a misnomer
all_issues_adjudicated: Prior knowledge sources for counterfactual conditioning are underspecified; their quality and potential biases directly affect the causal validity of generated counterfactuals
IS and FID metrics evaluate distributional similarity, not counterfactual fidelity; specific metrics for counterfactual attribute consistency are absent
Discriminator conditioning on counterfactual queries may be superficial; the paper does not verify that the discriminator genuinely enforces query-consistent generation
Diversity improvements may reflect spurious variation in non-counterfactual attributes rather than meaningful variation along the causally intervened dimension
The causal framework underpinning 'counterfactual' generation is not formally specified; without a structural causal model, the counterfactual label may be a misnomer

critic_raw:
Training Binary Neural Networks using the Bayesian Learning Rule proposes applying variational Bayes to optimize over binary-constrained weight distributions. A central methodological tension exists: true posterior inference over binary weights requires discrete distributions, but standard variational methods require differentiable approximations that relax the binary constraint. The paper's approach necessarily makes approximations that may introduce systematic bias. The gap between the variational approximation and the true binary posterior must be characterized theoretically or empirically to validate that Bayesian guarantees (uncertainty calibration, generalization bounds) transfer to the constrained setting.

The claim that Bayesian training 'enhances predictive performance' of BNNs compared to standard binary training must be evaluated carefully. Binary network performance is highly sensitive to architecture choices, dataset characteristics, and the specific binarization scheme. The paper must evaluate performance across multiple binary network architectures and compare against strong non-Bayesian BNN baselines (e.g., XNOR-Net, IR-Net) under matched hyperparameters and training budgets. The comparison may inadvertently benefit the Bayesian approach if baselines are not optimally tuned.

Computational efficiency is claimed as a virtue, but Bayesian optimization typically requires either Monte Carlo sampling (expensive at test time) or mean-field approximations (potentially inaccurate). For deployment in resource-constrained environments, the practical inference cost of the Bayesian BNN must be compared against deterministic binary inference, which is the primary motivation for using BNNs. If Bayesian inference requires floating-point uncertainty propagation at test time, it negates the binary efficiency advantage.

Uncertainty quantification quality should be validated using calibration metrics (Expected Calibration Error, reliability diagrams) and out-of-distribution detection performance. Without these evaluations, the claimed Bayesian benefits (better uncertainty quantification, generalization) are assertions rather than demonstrated properties. The paper appears to primarily report accuracy metrics, which do not validate the Bayesian rationale for the approach.
all_issues_raised: Variational approximation for discrete binary weights introduces systematic bias; gap between approximation and true binary posterior is not characterized
Performance comparisons may not use optimally tuned non-Bayesian BNN baselines (XNOR-Net, IR-Net, etc.) under matched training budgets
Bayesian inference may require floating-point operations at test time, negating the binary computation efficiency advantage central to BNN deployment motivation
Calibration metrics (ECE, reliability diagrams) and OOD detection are absent; claimed Bayesian uncertainty quantification benefits are not empirically validated
Binary weight posteriors may not provide meaningful uncertainty estimates due to the severe discretization; the Bayesian interpretation of binary weight distributions is not rigorously justified
all_issues_adjudicated: Variational approximation for discrete binary weights introduces systematic bias; gap between approximation and true binary posterior is not characterized
Performance comparisons may not use optimally tuned non-Bayesian BNN baselines (XNOR-Net, IR-Net, etc.) under matched training budgets
Bayesian inference may require floating-point operations at test time, negating the binary computation efficiency advantage central to BNN deployment motivation
Calibration metrics (ECE, reliability diagrams) and OOD detection are absent; claimed Bayesian uncertainty quantification benefits are not empirically validated
Binary weight posteriors may not provide meaningful uncertainty estimates due to the severe discretization; the Bayesian interpretation of binary weight distributions is not rigorously justified

critic_raw:
SCOUTER claims that its slot attention-based explanations outperform other methods on 'several explanation evaluation metrics.' The validity of this claim depends critically on what explanation evaluation metrics are used and whether they genuinely measure explanation quality versus proxy properties. Metrics such as pointing game accuracy or segmentation overlap with ground-truth annotations measure localization fidelity but not whether the identified regions are causally or counterfactually meaningful for the classifier's decision. The paper should clarify whether its evaluation metrics capture causal explanation quality or merely visual localization.

The claim that SCOUTER achieves 'similar classification accuracy to a fully connected model while performing better in confusion matrix metrics on a binary classification problem' is methodologically ambiguous. 'Similar' accuracy is not precisely defined (what tolerance constitutes similarity?), and confusion matrix metric superiority on a binary classification problem should be evaluated with statistical significance tests given that binary classification metrics can fluctuate substantially with class imbalance and threshold choice.

SCOUTER's slot attention module introduces an additional architectural component relative to standard classifiers. If SCOUTER's improved confusion matrix metrics come at the cost of increased model complexity, the improvement may reflect the additional capacity rather than the explainability mechanism. The paper should compare SCOUTER against a classifier with matched capacity but without slot attention to isolate the contribution of the explanation mechanism.

Positive and negative visual explanations are a distinctive feature of SCOUTER. The definition of 'negative explanation' (regions that reduce classifier confidence for a class) must be precisely operationalized and validated. Without a ground-truth notion of what constitutes a correct negative explanation, the evaluation of negative explanation quality is inherently subjective and potentially self-referential.
all_issues_raised: Explanation evaluation metrics may measure visual localization rather than causal or counterfactual explanation quality; the distinction is not clearly established
Classification accuracy similarity is undefined in terms of tolerance; confusion matrix metric superiority lacks statistical significance testing
SCOUTER's improved metrics may reflect additional model capacity from slot attention rather than the explanation mechanism itself; capacity-matched baselines are absent
Negative explanation quality evaluation lacks a ground-truth operationalization; assessment may be subjective and self-referential
Slot attention is stochastic (assignments can vary across runs); reproducibility of specific slot assignments and explanation consistency across runs is not addressed
all_issues_adjudicated: Explanation evaluation metrics may measure visual localization rather than causal or counterfactual explanation quality; the distinction is not clearly established
Classification accuracy similarity is undefined in terms of tolerance; confusion matrix metric superiority lacks statistical significance testing
SCOUTER's improved metrics may reflect additional model capacity from slot attention rather than the explanation mechanism itself; capacity-matched baselines are absent
Negative explanation quality evaluation lacks a ground-truth operationalization; assessment may be subjective and self-referential
Slot attention is stochastic (assignments can vary across runs); reproducibility of specific slot assignments and explanation consistency across runs is not addressed

critic_raw:
The SKS multi-task learning framework for hate speech detection combines hate speech classification with sentiment analysis as an auxiliary task. The theoretical motivation for this pairing rests on the assumption that sentiment and hate speech are positively correlated tasks that share useful representations. This assumption may not hold universally: non-hateful text can be strongly negative in sentiment (critical reviews, news reporting on tragedy), while some hate speech may be expressed neutrally. The paper should verify empirically that the sentiment and hate speech labels are sufficiently correlated in the evaluation datasets to justify multi-task sharing.

The 'category embeddings' derived from a dictionary of derogatory words are an additional knowledge injection mechanism that introduces a lexical bias: if the derogatory word dictionary is incomplete or domain-specific, the model will fail to detect hate speech that uses novel, evolved, or coded language not present in the dictionary. The paper should characterize the dictionary coverage and evaluate model performance on hate speech instances that do not rely on lexically flagged terms.

Multi-task learning with sentiment introduces a label dependency issue: if hate speech annotations were performed by annotators who were also sensitive to sentiment cues, the hate speech labels may themselves be partially explained by sentiment, creating a circular validation when sentiment improves hate speech classification metrics. The paper should use hate speech datasets where annotation protocols specifically controlled for this conflation.

The multi-head attention architecture's performance gains over single-task baselines should be decomposed to separately quantify the contribution of: (1) the multi-head attention architecture itself, (2) the multi-task sentiment objective, and (3) the category embeddings. Without this ablation, it is impossible to determine which component drives improvements and whether the full system's complexity is justified.
all_issues_raised: Theoretical motivation for sentiment-hate speech multi-task pairing assumes positive correlation that does not always hold; empirical validation of label correlation is needed
Category embeddings from derogatory word dictionaries create lexical bias; performance on hate speech without lexically flagged terms is not evaluated
Circular validation risk: if hate speech annotations were influenced by sentiment, sentiment-guided improvement is partly tautological
No ablation decomposing contributions of (1) multi-head attention architecture, (2) sentiment multi-task objective, and (3) category embeddings independently
Generalizability of findings across hate speech datasets with different annotation protocols and demographic coverage is not assessed
all_issues_adjudicated: Theoretical motivation for sentiment-hate speech multi-task pairing assumes positive correlation that does not always hold; empirical validation of label correlation is needed
Category embeddings from derogatory word dictionaries create lexical bias; performance on hate speech without lexically flagged terms is not evaluated
Circular validation risk: if hate speech annotations were influenced by sentiment, sentiment-guided improvement is partly tautological
No ablation decomposing contributions of (1) multi-head attention architecture, (2) sentiment multi-task objective, and (3) category embeddings independently
Generalizability of findings across hate speech datasets with different annotation protocols and demographic coverage is not assessed

critic_raw:
Fair Selective Classification via Sufficiency introduces a mutual information-based regularizer to enforce sufficiency fairness — the property that classifier outputs are conditionally independent of the sensitive attribute given the true label. However, mutual information estimation in high-dimensional settings is notoriously difficult, and the chosen estimator's bias, variance, and convergence properties under finite samples are not characterized. If the estimator is biased or high-variance, the regularizer may not reliably enforce sufficiency in practice, particularly for small minority subgroups.

The method's claim that it 'does not increase fairness disparities compared to a baseline selective classification model' is a relative claim that depends critically on the baseline's own fairness properties. If the baseline already has poor fairness characteristics, a method that does not worsen them may still produce unacceptable disparities in absolute terms. The paper should report absolute disparity measures, not only relative comparisons, to characterize the practical fairness improvement achieved.

Selective classification introduces an abstention mechanism that can itself be discriminatory: if the model abstains disproportionately on minority subgroups, coverage disparities create a form of disparate impact even when precision is equalized among covered samples. The paper's sufficiency criterion focuses on precision fairness among classified samples but may not address coverage fairness across subgroups. Both dimensions should be evaluated jointly.

The evaluation on four datasets with 'unique types of data' provides breadth, but the sufficiency criterion's enforceability depends on whether the sensitive attribute is known and well-measured in each dataset. If sensitive attribute labels are noisy or socially contested, the mutual information regularizer is optimizing against a noisy signal, and the resulting fairness guarantees are weaker than claimed. The paper should assess robustness to sensitive attribute label noise.
all_issues_raised: Mutual information estimator's bias and variance under finite samples are not characterized; reliable sufficiency enforcement for small minority subgroups is not guaranteed
Fairness comparisons are relative to baseline; absolute disparity measures are needed to assess whether resulting disparities are practically acceptable
Coverage disparity (differential abstention rates across subgroups) is a form of disparate impact not addressed by the precision-focused sufficiency criterion
Robustness to noisy or imprecise sensitive attribute labels is not evaluated; noisy labels weaken mutual information regularizer guarantees
Sufficiency criterion may conflict with accuracy-preserving objectives under distribution shift; no evaluation of fairness-accuracy tradeoffs across coverage scales in realistic deployment scenarios
all_issues_adjudicated: Mutual information estimator's bias and variance under finite samples are not characterized; reliable sufficiency enforcement for small minority subgroups is not guaranteed
Fairness comparisons are relative to baseline; absolute disparity measures are needed to assess whether resulting disparities are practically acceptable
Coverage disparity (differential abstention rates across subgroups) is a form of disparate impact not addressed by the precision-focused sufficiency criterion
Robustness to noisy or imprecise sensitive attribute labels is not evaluated; noisy labels weaken mutual information regularizer guarantees
Sufficiency criterion may conflict with accuracy-preserving objectives under distribution shift; no evaluation of fairness-accuracy tradeoffs across coverage scales in realistic deployment scenarios

critic_raw:
FamNet's few-shot counting approach uses exemplar objects from within the same image to adapt to novel categories at test time. The adaptation strategy's effectiveness depends on the quality and representativeness of the provided exemplars. When exemplars are atypical instances of the target object (e.g., partially occluded, unusual viewpoint), the adaptation will produce poor counting estimates. The paper does not characterize how exemplar quality affects performance, nor does it provide guidance on exemplar selection. A rigorous evaluation should test performance across multiple exemplar selection strategies and report sensitivity to exemplar quality.

The FSC-147 dataset is introduced as a new benchmark alongside the model, creating a methodological conflict of interest: the benchmark was presumably designed with the model's capabilities in mind (or vice versa), and the model may be implicitly overfitted to the statistical properties of FSC-147's images and object categories. The paper should evaluate FamNet on pre-existing few-shot counting benchmarks, or at minimum provide an analysis of how FSC-147 was designed to be representative of a broader counting challenge distribution.

Comparison against object detectors and other counting methods is only valid when all compared methods are given the same information at inference time. If FamNet receives the exemplar objects as additional input while object detectors operate without this guidance, the comparison is not controlled for input information. The paper should ensure that comparisons are conducted under matched input conditions or clearly disclose information asymmetries.

The test-time adaptation strategy modifies the model using the test image and exemplars, which means FamNet is performing transductive inference rather than purely inductive inference. This is methodologically distinct from standard few-shot learning evaluation and should be clearly labeled as such. Models that do not use test-time adaptation should not be compared directly without acknowledging this asymmetry.
all_issues_raised: Exemplar quality sensitivity is not characterized; adaptation performance when exemplars are atypical or low-quality is not evaluated
FSC-147 dataset is introduced by the same authors as FamNet, creating potential overfitting of model design to benchmark statistical properties
Comparisons against object detectors and counting methods may not control for input information asymmetry (exemplar availability versus non-availability)
Test-time adaptation constitutes transductive inference; direct comparison to inductive baselines without acknowledging this asymmetry is methodologically misleading
Claim of generalization to novel visual categories is tested only on FSC-147 categories; cross-dataset generalization to independently collected counting benchmarks is absent
all_issues_adjudicated: Exemplar quality sensitivity is not characterized; adaptation performance when exemplars are atypical or low-quality is not evaluated
FSC-147 dataset is introduced by the same authors as FamNet, creating potential overfitting of model design to benchmark statistical properties
Comparisons against object detectors and counting methods may not control for input information asymmetry (exemplar availability versus non-availability)
Test-time adaptation constitutes transductive inference; direct comparison to inductive baselines without acknowledging this asymmetry is methodologically misleading
Claim of generalization to novel visual categories is tested only on FSC-147 categories; cross-dataset generalization to independently collected counting benchmarks is absent
